hero_summary: Maqam Iraq belongs to the Sikah family, but it should not be confused with Sikah itself. Its tonic is Iraq (B half-flat), not Sikah (E half-flat), so it belongs to the Sikah world in color and genus, while differing in tonic, lower structure, and sayr.
short_identity: A Sikah-family maqam whose tonic is Iraq (B half-flat), built in its documented form from Jins Sikah on Iraq followed by Jins Bayati on Dukah (D).

quick_facts.character: A Sikah-family maqam with its own tonic and lower structure
quick_facts.common_start: It begins from Iraq (B half-flat), then expands toward Dukah (D) and above, before returning to the tonic

sayr.summary: Maqam Iraq belongs to the Sikah family, yet it must not be confused with Sikah itself. Its maqamic identity rests on the fact that its tonic is Iraq (B half-flat), not Sikah (E half-flat). The sources that could be verified agree in classifying it among maqamat built on Iraq inside the Sikah family. In the documented theoretical description, it consists of Jins Sikah/Segah on Iraq (B half-flat), followed by Jins Bayati on Dukah (D).
resting_tones: Iraq (B half-flat): the tonic and final resting point
Dukah (D): an important upper resting point because it carries the second jins in the documented structure
Its auditory color remains connected to the Sikah sphere, but the ultimate point of attraction remains Iraq, not Sikah
motion_notes: Its sayr may be ascending or ascending-descending
It begins from Iraq (B half-flat) in the lower region, then opens toward Dukah (D) and above, before returning to the tonic
The listener must never lose the sense that Iraq is the original tonic, because this is what distinguishes it from nearby Sikah-family maqamat
Its identity emerges more through phrase and motion than through a classroom scalar diagram alone
common_path: Establish the Sikah color on Iraq (B half-flat)
Return convincingly to Iraq as tonic

common_modulations: Sikah and Iraq belong to the same family, but Sikah centers on Sikah (E half-flat), whereas Iraq resolves on Iraq (B half-flat); this is a structural difference, not a cosmetic one

Huzam also belongs to the Sikah family, but usually centers on Sikah (E half-flat) and is commonly explained through Hijaz on Nawa, whereas Iraq has a different tonic and a different documented structure
A documented study notes that Iraq shares its scale with Awj, but that does not make them the same maqam; the difference lies in sayr, emphasis, and cadence

examples.title: Qul lilladhi bil-bu'd wal-hijr jazana
examples.note: Cited in Andalusian/Maghribi material as a piece corresponding fully to Eastern Maqam Iraq

examples.title: Yaqullu lakthahu zaman al-azhar al-dunya maliha
examples.note: Also cited from the same Iraq nawba material as matching Eastern Maqam Iraq

reference_recordings.title: Qul lilladhi bil-bu'd wal-hijr jazana
reference_recordings.note: A direct textual example from Iraq nawba material
